primary_diagnosis.name: Acute health-anxiety/panic with tension headache, on a background of mild immune thrombocytopenia (platelets ~75); no bleeding

pathophysiology_summary: Immune thrombocytopenia is an autoimmune reduction in platelets; the spontaneous bleeding risk is closely tied to the count, and serious spontaneous bleeding (including intracranial haemorrhage) is rare until the platelet count falls well below ~10-20 x10^9/L. A count of ~75 is comfortably in the safe range and is typically monitored without treatment. Here, catastrophic misreading of the result produced a panic attack with a band-like tension headache, tachycardia and hyperventilation, and fear of a brain bleed. The mucocutaneous 'wet vs dry' assessment (petechiae/purpura, oral/mucosal bleeding) and a focal-neurology/thunderclap-headache screen — much of which is assessable on video and by history — allow safe reassurance, with a bleeding/neurological safety-net and routine haematology follow-up.

differential.evidence_basis: Band-like headache and adrenergic symptoms starting on reading a mildly abnormal result, with prior health anxiety.
Screen for thunderclap headache, focal deficit, vomiting; absent, and unlikely at a count of ~75.
Screen for petechiae/wet purpura that precede major bleeding; absent, and the count is ~75.
No bone pain, organomegaly, anaemia or systemic toxicity.

No mucocutaneous bleeding (DI-001), band-like non-thunderclap headache with no focal deficit/vomiting (DI-002), trigger-locked onset with a safe count (~75) and prior anxiety (DI-003) — a panic/tension picture.
No thunderclap headache, focal deficit or vomiting; very low risk at a count of ~75 — retained safety-net.
No petechiae/wet purpura; count ~75 is in the safe range.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct output: accurate reassurance that ~75 is a safe count, mucocutaneous/neurology screen, treat the tension headache, and routine GP/haematology follow-up, with a bleeding/neurological safety-net. Both reflex ambulance and unscreened dismissal are errors.

red_flags.description: Mucocutaneous bleeding — petechiae/purpura, new bruising, epistaxis, gum/urinary/GI bleeding (wet purpura)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Wet purpura signals higher bleeding risk.

red_flags.description: Intracranial haemorrhage — thunderclap 'worst ever' headache, focal weakness/speech change, or vomiting
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed intracranial haemorrhage is catastrophic.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being drawn into the 'brain bleed' fear and dispatching an ambulance for a safe count with no bleeding or neurology.
Reassuring purely because it 'sounds anxious' without a mucocutaneous/neurology screen.

cannot_determine_via_chat: A current platelet count and full blood film
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Reassure (count ~75 is safe) with a mucocutaneous/neurology screen; treat the tension headache; routine GP/haematology follow-up; bleeding/neurological safety-net.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: False reassurance could miss a rare bleed; over-triage reinforces catastrophic health anxiety.
